items.title: Ocean warming weakens the sea–land breeze in coastal megacities
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 17 April 2026

Ocean warming weakens the sea–land breeze in coastal megacities
                    Yunting Xiao 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-3489-89581, Yaxin Liu1, Yu Nie 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-7019-04422, Yongjie Fang3, Hao Liu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-8242-19061, Yan Dou1, Jianbo Yang4,5, Cong-Qiang Liu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-7451-09921, Pingqing Fu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-6249-22801 & …Jialei Zhu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-2057-89961 Show authors

Nature Climate Change

(2026)Cite this article

Subjects
        
            Climate and Earth system modellingSustainability

AbstractThe sea–land breeze (SLB), driven by thermal contrasts between sea and land, forms a key circulation system in coastal cities which mitigates urban heat islands, improves air quality and supports urban liveability. Yet its response to rising sea-surface temperatures (SST) under global warming remains unclear. Here we simulate SLB evolution across 18 major coastal megacities under varying SST. The results show that historical SST increases have reduced SLB days by 3–45% in 67% of these cities, with mid-latitude cities experiencing the largest declines (29–45%) as a result of a more than 5% reduction in diurnal sea–land thermal contrast. A further 0.52 °C (~2%) SST increase relative to historical levels induces a 4.5-fold reduction in SLB days in high-impact regions and heightened sensitivity in moderate-impact regions under high-emission scenarios. This erosion of SLB days, primarily driven by ocean warming, poses a critical yet overlooked threat to coastal liveability.

MainMore than 50% of the global urban population resides within 100 km of the coast where populations still rapidly grow1. In these coastal cities, the sea–land breeze (SLB) is the most prominent thermally driven local circulation feature owing to the different heat capacities of continents and marine surfaces, thus playing a crucial role in the urban climate and environment. By transporting cooler marine air inland and enhancing boundary-layer mixing, SLB can mitigate summertime urban warming, improve ventilation and shape key components of coastal urban liveability, including thermal comfort and air quality2,3. Ongoing global climate change through altering the thermal conditions of the oceans influences the intensity and characteristics of the SLB4,5. Change in SLB characteristics serves as a crucial link between urgent climate change action (sustainable development goal (SDG) 13) and urban sustainability (SDG 11). However, the impact of global warming on SLB in coastal cities remains unknown.In recent years, the characteristics of SLB have undergone unprecedented changes in the context of global warming. An SLB day is defined as a day dominated by a thermally driven circulation, during which winds alternate between seaward and landward directions in the absence of large-scale synoptic forcing. It typically manifests as sea breezes during daytime and land breezes at night. The number of SLB days is one of the key indicators of the extent to which a city benefits from SLB circulation6, whose variations exhibit large regional differences. Previous observations indicate that the number of SLB days decreases from south to north along China’s eastern coastal cities7. Low-latitude regions generally experience more SLB days than do high-latitude regions8. These spatial patterns are probably influenced by the distribution and variation of SST9,10. The ongoing global warming has led to rising SST, the trend that is projected to persist11,12. Predicting future changes in SLB days under constant SST rise caused by various human activities is pivotal for developing effective climate adaptation strategies.The continuous elevation of global SST has obviously altered regional climate conditions and the frequency of meteorological disasters, posing a particular threat to the liveability of coastal cities. Climbing SST exacerbates the urban heat island effect, especially in summer13,14. The heat from the warming ocean combined with anthropogenic heat from urban areas further amplifies extreme heat events in coastal cities15. Enhanced evaporation due to increased SST raises atmospheric moisture, resulting in more frequent heavy rainfall and urban flooding16,17. Increased SST leads to a rising occurrence of intense typhoons causing severe damage to urban areas18,19. Additionally, abnormal increases in SST inevitably alters the temperature gradient between land and sea20, affecting SLB formation. Changes in the number of SLB days should be regarded as a key consequence of SST rise, yet the specific mechanisms and effects require further investigation.Temperature, humidity, air quality and urban ventilation jointly shape the liveability of coastal cities21. Sea breezes bring cool air that helps alleviate urban heat and maintain a comfortable temperature range22. They also carry moisture to increase the humidity in coastal cities, while land breezes generally remove moisture, leading to dry urban centres23,24. Sea breezes help disperse pollutants such as vehicle emissions and industrial discharges, improving air quality23. Moderate sea breezes improve urban ventilation, reduce heat retention and enhance comfort in hot humid regions25. Given the growing impact of climate change, understanding future shifts in SLB patterns is essential for safeguarding coastal urban liveability.It is of great importance to clarify the impacts and mechanisms of rising SST on SLB days in coastal cities across different regions globally, as well as its response to human activities. We developed a regional weather model incorporating both day-to-day and 6-h SST variations to investigate how rising SST affects SLB days in 18 major coastal megacities worldwide, across different climate zones and latitudes. Model evaluation in this study is primarily conducted against the ERA5 reanalysis, focusing on the identification and variability of SLB days for the analysed periods. Our findings reveal that the number of SLB days in mid-latitude coastal megacities is more sensitive to SST change than megacities at low latitudes. While the decline in SLB days associated with rising SST may be challenging to reverse, proactive climate actions could mitigate or slow this trend across coastal megacities. Mitigating global warming can help sustain SLB days, thereby enhancing the liveability of coastal cities.Historical SST rise reduces SLB daysA total of 18 coastal megacities with populations exceeding 140 million, spanning low latitudes and mid-latitudes between 65° N and 65° S were selected (Methods) to examine the effect of SST change on the number of SLB days. These megacities represent all climate types except that in polar regions, and have been previously identified as highly influenced by SLB activity8 (Supplementary Table 1). A weather research and forecasting model (WRF) was developed to incorporate both day-to-day and 6-h SST variations. Both reanalysis result and observational evidence consistently indicate a decline in the number of SLB days across most coastal megacities examined in this study (Supplementary Text 1). Validation in the main text is primarily based on ERA5 and focuses on SLB-day occurrence for the analysed periods. Sensitivity experiments comparing SST conditions between 2010 and 1970 show that in 12 of the 18 megacities, SLB days decline as SST rises. Global SST changes exhibit regional variability (Fig. 1). In the coast of tropical megacities such as Mumbai, Jakarta, Douala, Maputo and Dubai, SST has increased by 0.25 °C to 0.44 °C, typically below 0.60 °C (5%), reflecting a relatively modest warming trend. In contrast, the coastal waters of mid-latitude megacities have experienced a larger relative SST increase. London, New York and Buenos Aires have seen SST rise by over 0.18 °C (10%). The temperature increase in Tokyo was ~1.30 °C (4%), while that in Los Angeles was 1.08 °C (3%). Although the absolute SST increase in these mid-latitude coastal waters (0.19 °C to 0.32 °C) is generally lower than tropical regions, the relative warming is more substantial. Basemap data from GADM (https://gadm.org/).A total of 67% of the coastal megacities experienced a decline in the number of SLB days as SST increased, but this response was not homogeneous. In mid-latitude coastal megacities, such as London, New York, Shanghai, Tianjin, Lisbon and Buenos Aires, SLB days consistently decreased with rising SST. On average, SST in these regions increased by 0.73 °C (9%), resulting in ~40% reduction in the number of SLB days. In low-latitude megacities, the response of the number of SLB days to rising SST is weaker than that in mid-latitude megacities. Although these megacities have roughly 40 more SLB days per year than mid-latitude megacities, rising SST has led to a nearly 17% reduction in SLB days. Some tropical coastal megacities, such as Douala, Dubai, Jakarta and Maputo, even exhibited a 1% to 16% increase in SLB days with rising SST. This trend aligns with the weaker SST rise observed in low-latitude regions (~2%) compared with the more pronounced increase in mid-latitudes (~9%). The SST increase causes a larger reduction in SLB days during summer and autumn (~47%) than that in spring and winter (~24%) in mid-latitude coastal megacities. (Supplementary Fig. 1).Driven by reduced sea–land thermal contrastUnsupervised machine learning technique (Methods) was applied to classify the 18 coastal megacities in this study into three distinct subsets based on the changes in SLB days in response to the SST increase (Fig. 2a). High-impact regions (HIRs; red dots in Fig. 2a) include seven megacities, which are New York, London, Shanghai, Lisbon, Shenzhen, Tianjin and Buenos Aires. The surrounding ocean of these megacities suffers the largest increase in SST, ranging from 0.13 °C to 1.3 °C, resulting in the most pronounced declines in SLB days among the three subsets. Integrated analysis of meteorological stations from both land and marine environments reveals a fluctuating yet declining trend in the number of SLB days across the four coastal megacities within HIRs (Supplementary Text 1 and Extended Data Fig. 1). New York and Lisbon saw reductions of more than 30%, while London and Shanghai experienced declines exceeding 40% due to SST rising between 1970 and 2010. HIRs situated in subtropical or temperate zones, experience SST increases exceeding 0.75 °C (5%) and exhibit high urbanization levels (Fig. 1). Within these regions, thermal variability from terrestrial sources remains stable, while SLB frequency is notably susceptible to SST changes. SLB days in HIRs declined most sharply in autumn (−51%) and least in spring (−23%), highlighting seasonal differences in SST-induced impacts. (Supplementary Fig. 1a). Another three megacities situated at the mid-latitude—Tokyo, Cape Town and Rio de Janeiro—are classified as moderate-impact regions (MIRs, blue dots in Fig. 2a). The defining characteristic of this group is that the change in SLB days is less pronounced when compared with that observed in the previously described HIR megacities. Tokyo experienced a reduction of about 12%, while Rio de Janeiro and Cape Town saw declines of around 20%. The SST variations in the surrounding coastal waters of these megacities are also relatively small, about 0.65 °C (4%) on average. Although the changes in SLB days are currently small for megacities in the MIRs, this is probably caused by the relatively small SST variations in their surrounding ocean. As for HIRs, over 50% of annual SLB anomalies in MIRs are attributed to the decrease in summer and autumn (Supplementary Fig. 1b). The other eight megacities (marked with green dots in Fig. 2a), are defined as the low-impact regions (LIRs). In these megacities, the reduction in SLB days is minimal (less than 10%) or, in some cases, even slightly increases with rise in SST in the surrounding ocean temperature. In sites such as Miami and Mumbai, rising SST has led to a slight decrease in SLB days (~3%), while Maputo, Melbourne, Dubai and Jakarta have seen a small increase in SLB days (~4%) due to increase in SST. The relationship between SLB days and SST changes in LIR megacities remains inconsistent.Fig. 2: Clustering of variations in SLB days sensitivity to SST anomalies and the underlying mechanism.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea, Clustering variations in SLB days sensitivity to SST anomalies. Cluster analysis categorizes the data into HIRs (red dots), LIRs (brown dots) and MIRs (blue dots). b–d, The contributions of influencing factors, including SLWF, SLCI and SLTD, to decreased SLB days in LIRs (b), MIRs (c) and HIRs (d). Change in SLTD is the primary factor reducing the number of SLB days in both HIRs and MIRs.We integrated key criteria influencing SLB to understand the meteorological mechanisms for the decline of SLB days (Methods). Three critical factors were identified, including the sea–land temperature difference (SLTD), the sea–land wind field (SLWF) and the sea–land circulation intensity (SLCI). These factors are logically interconnected in sequential order. SLTD refers to the average temperature difference between surrounding ocean and land, which is the thermodynamic driver of SLB26,27. SLWF includes wind speed and wind direction in coastal area, acting as the direct indicator to identify SLB circulation8,28. SLCI refers to the frequency (>4 h) of sea breeze and land breeze within 24 h, reflecting the duration of SLB circulation2,29. It is assessed once the wind direction is established. The contribution of each factor to the reduction in SLB days was assessed by quantifying the decrease in SLB days caused by each factor. SLTD is the dominant factor driving the decline in the HIR megacities (Fig. 2d). In all HIR megacities, except Buenos Aires, the reduction in SLB days due to changes in temperature difference exceeds 50%, with London, Tianjin and Shenzhen showing temperature difference induced declines of 65% or more. An average 0.43 °C (8%) rise in SST, compared with a modest 0.15 °C (0.2%) increase in land surface temperature, reduced the daytime land–sea thermal contrast and consequently weakened sea breeze development (Supplementary Text 2). Driven by ocean currents and anthropogenic climate change, mid-latitude marine regions exhibit larger increases in SST during autumn and summer12,30,31, leading to the large reduction of SLB day in the autumn and summer. Temperature difference remains the primary factor driving the reduction in SLB days in the MIRs. However, because of the relatively small increase in SST (<5%) in this area, the decline in SLB days is minimal (Fig. 2c). The annual mean SST is 21.28 °C in MIRs, with an increase of 0.47 °C, while the annual mean SST in HIRs is 17.88 °C with an increase of 0.77 °C. The contributions of SLTD to the change in SLB day of LIR megacities are much smaller compared with HIRs and MIRs, while the contributions of SLCI largely increase. The sea breeze lasted an average of 5.3 h, but this duration decreases to 3.5 h when SST rises in LIRs (Fig. 2b).Weak emission controls accelerate SLB declineGlobal climate change has led to rising SST and projected to continue. We further investigate the impact of future SST rise under different shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) on SLB days in coastal megacities. SSP 245 represents a moderate emission scenario with a projected global temperature rise of about 2.50 °C–3.00 °C above pre-industrial levels. Integrated results from the coupled model intercomparison project phase 6 (CMIP6) models consistently project a global average SST increase of 0.75 °C by 2050 relative to 2010, corresponding to an ~4% rise under the SSP 245 scenario (Fig. 3). In comparison, the global average SST is projected to rise by 1.28 °C by 2050 under the SSP 585 scenario, which is 1.5 times greater than under SSP 245. Under the SSP 245 scenario, average SSTs are lowest in the HIRs (18.36 °C), moderate in the MIRs (21.19 °C) and highest in the LIRs (24.43 °C). The greatest warming occurred in the LIRs with an increase of 0.86 °C (~4%), followed by the HIRs with 0.71 °C (~4%), whereas the MIRs showed the smallest rise of 0.44 °C (~2%). Under the SSP 585 scenario, HIRs remains the regions with the lowest average SST but experiences the largest increase of 2.22 °C (8%) by 2050. MIRs experience an average ocean warming of 1.5 °C (4%), with an increase in SST by 0.41 °C higher than the increase under the SSP 245 scenario. Owing to high average SST, LIRs exhibit a small warming increase of 1.34 °C (5%), with little difference between SSP 585 and SSP 245 scenario.Fig. 3: Projected changes in SLB days and percentages for 18 coastal megacities by 2050 under future emission scenarios.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imageThe red bar charts show a decrease in SLB days whereas the blue bar charts show an increase. Basemap data from GADM (https://gadm.org/).Although the SSP 245 scenario includes anthropogenic emission reduction to mitigate global warming, the slight increase in SST still leads to a decline in the number of SLB days in 15 out of the 18 coastal megacities examined in this study by 2050 compared with 2010. Fortunately, the reduction in SLB days is generally modest, with the largest decline observed in Shenzhen at 13%. Coastal SSTs rise by 0.71 °C (~4%) relative to historical levels under the SSP 245 scenario, resulting in a 6% average (range from 1% to 13%) reduction in SLB days across all HIR megacities. This indicates that even a slight future rise in SST could still lead to a decrease in SLB days in HIRs. The simulations driven by SST from five (ACCESS-ESM1.532, BCC-CSM2-MR, CanESM533, CESM234 and EC-Earth335) widely recognized global climate models (GCMs) exhibit high consistency, underscoring the robustness of the projections and providing consistent evidence that rising SST is a major factor contributing to the observed reduction in SLB days (Supplementary Text 3 and Supplementary Fig. 2). The surrounding ocean experienced a relatively small increase in SSTs, resulting in a slight decline in SLB days (0–5%) across the three MIR megacities. Changes in SLB days are highly variable for LIRs. Some megacities (for example, Maputo, Melbourne, Dubai, Los Angeles and Douala) see a decline of 2–7%, while others (for example, Miami, Mumbai and Jakarta) experience an increase of 2–13%. This aligns with our above finding that SLB-day changes in LIR megacities exhibit weak sensitivity and an inconsistent response to SST variations.Further analysis of the contributions of the three key factors influencing SLB days in HIR megacities reveals that, under the SSP 245 scenario, all HIR megacities except for New York exhibit a smaller contribution from SLTD compared with the historical experiment (compare Fig. 2d with Fig. 4a). This may explain why the sensitivity of SLB-day reduction to SST changes is lower than in the historical experiment, leading to a 28% less decrease in SLB days of HIR megacities under SSP 245. In contrast, moderately elevated SST under SSP 245—despite reduced anthropogenic emissions—suppresses sea breeze development by shortening its duration and weakening SLB intensity, leading to a 10% increase in SLCI contribution compared with the historical period resulting in decreasing SLB days in HIRs (Fig. 4a). With the extent of SST rise being mitigated, the influence of the SLTD on the number of SLB days in MIRs is further diminished with increased contribution from SLCI as in HIRs (compare Fig. 2c and Fig. 4c). The contribution of SLTD is inherently minimal in LIRs when the increase in SST is mitigated (compare Fig. 2a and Supplementary Fig. 1a). Less SST increase moderates the decline in SLB days across HIR megacities under SSP 245. Under rapid warming driven by high-emission scenarios, changes in SLTD contribute more to the reduction in SLB days over coastal megacities compared with low-emission scenarios.Under the SSP 585 scenario, a high emissions pathway, global average SST is 0.41 °C (2%) higher than the SSP 245 scenario. However, SLB days in HIR megacities will decrease by 10–60% by 2050 under the SSP 585 scenario compared with 2010, with an average decline of 27%, almost 4.5 times greater than under the SSP 245 scenario (6%) (Fig. 3). By 2050, the average SST in the surrounding ocean of HIRs will increase by 1.03 °C compared with 2010, only 0.76 °C higher than under the SSP 245 scenario. This highlights the strongly amplified impact of the SST rise on SLB-day reductions, where even a small further increase in SST will cause much larger reductions in SLB days. SLB-day reductions in the three MIR megacities are modest under both the historical and SSP 245 scenarios. However, under SSP 585, SST in the coastal areas of MIRs increases by 0.85 °C by 2050, which is 0.41 °C higher than in SSP 245, resulting in an additional reduction of 18 to 33 SLB days, comparable to or greater than the decrease in HIRs. The temperature difference plays an increasingly important role in diminishing SLB days in MIRs (Fig. 4b,d). SLTD contributes over 50% of the reduction in SLB days within HIRs and MIRs except for Rio de Janeiro under SSP 585. Compared with the SSP 245 scenario, the average contribution of temperature difference increased from 51% to 63%, while the contribution of SLB intensity declined from 12% to 7% for HIRs under the high-emission scenario (compare Fig. 4a,b). MIRs experienced a marked increase in the contribution of temperature difference (18% to 53%), coinciding with a decrease in intensity effects (38% to 16%) (Fig. 4c,d).Building on idealized experiments, simulations forced with realistic future climate scenarios (Methods) confirm a marked decrease in SLB days. The decline in SLB days persists when the regional model is driven by scenario-consistent initial and boundary conditions, reinforcing our main conclusion that SST-driven warming tends to suppress SLB day occurrence across coastal megacities. The SLB days in seven HIRs are projected to experience a reduction of 6.1% under SSP 245 attributed to the decrease in air temperature difference between the ocean and the land mostly, which is intensified to 23.9% under SSP 585 (Supplementary Text 4 and Extended Data Fig. 2). The sluggish implementation of emission constraints will largely exacerbate the reduction of SLB days in HIRs, while increasing the sensitivity of coastal megacities in MIRs to ocean warming, pushing them towards HIR-like conditions.DiscussionThis study enhances SLB simulation in coastal megacities through incorporating dynamic SST forcing into a regional weather model and resolving day-to-day and 6-h thermal gradient variability, thereby reducing the limitations of conventional static SST parameterizations. Our research indicates that rising SST reduces SLB days in over 60% of megacities under various climate scenarios, primarily as a result of reduced SLTD in highly sensitive regions. The ongoing rise in global SST could lead to a much larger reduction in SLB days in more coastal megacities.Rising SST induces changes in land surface temperature over coastal megacities, which in turn modulate the sea–land thermal contrast and drive variations in the number of SLB days. However, the specific nature of these changes depends on the distinct SLB characteristics of each coastal megacity. (Supplementary Text 2 and Supplementary Fig. 3). Urbanization evidently also plays a critical role in influencing SLB dynamics in coastal megacities, with effects that are complex and bidirectional. The combined effects of the urban heat island and dense built structures can intensify the land–sea thermal contrast, promoting the development of SLB36,37. The increased surface roughness and boundary effects stemming from urban sprawl tend to diminish the transport dynamics of these breezes38. In addition, urban morphology in coastal megacities has largely stabilized over time and remains resistant to substantial change now and in the future, particularly as the pace of urbanization has slowed in most megacities. By contrast, the persistent rise in global SST presents a more pressing challenge. It is expected to exert a greater impact on SLB dynamics in coastal megacities than urbanization, as it directly affects regional temperatures and sea–land thermal contrasts. While the effects of urbanization on SLB are partially modifiable through policy and planning, the effects of rising SST are more profound and far less amenable to mitigation.Rising SST exacerbates liveability degradation in coastal megacities by impairing SLB, thereby threatening SDG 13 (climate action) and SDG 11 (sustainable cities). Emerging evidence suggests that future SST warming and El Niño/Southern Oscillation variability could substantially modulate offshore wind power generation39,40. The projected weakening of SLB circulation may further reduce wind energy availability over coastal regions, implying a potential decline in wind power potential under future climate scenarios. SLB enhances thermal comfort through humidity regulation, urban heat island mitigation and pollutant dispersion, yet persistent SST amplification reduces SLB intensity and frequency (−27% under SSP 585), intensifying urban heat stress. Current climate policies neglect this critical feedback, escalating risks to urban habitability and public health. High-resolution modelling of the SST–SLB–thermal comfort nexus provides practical insights for evidence-based governance in global coastal megacities. The anticipated decline in SLB circulation demands proactive urban adaptation. Countering reduces natural ventilation and increased heat stress in coastal megacities requires preserving coastal ventilation corridors and strategically designing built forms to channel sea breezes. Embedding these measures into urban policy is essential to maintain thermal comfort and resilience amid rapid sea-surface warming. Consequently, climate action must be implemented to mitigate SST rise and ensure sustainable thermal comfort in coastal megacities.MethodsSST coercionConventional low-resolution SST datasets fail to resolve localized warming disparities in nearshore zones, whereas kilometre-scale resolution data delineate land–sea thermal gradients with <0.1 °C precision, pinpointing SLB attenuation thresholds to inform climate-resilient urban planning. Existing WRF implementations with static SST fields create oversimplified marine thermal profiles, where persistent 24-h SST averaging degrades land–sea atmosphere coupling simulations in nearshore zones. This study integrates BCC-CSM2-MR CMIP6 SST outputs to characterize diurnally varying thermal signatures across marine domains adjacent to 18 coastal megacities, addressing inherent limitations of coarse-resolution SST inputs and absence of diurnal variability of WRF. Different GCM exhibit varied performance in SST simulations (Supplementary Fig. 4). Compared with observations, we find that the SST from BCC-CSM2-MR shows strong consistency with both reanalysis datasets and satellite assimilation data in capturing monthly variation of SST among several CMIP6 GCM (ACCESS-ESM1.5, BCC-CSM2-MR, CanESM5, CESM2 and EC-Earth3) (Supplementary Fig. 5). Furthermore, statistical analyses confirm that BCC-CSM2-MR results demonstrate high reliability across several metrics (Supplementary Text 5).This study advances oceanic surface temperature parameterization of WRF model through the following technical workflow. Using the MODIS land-use classification system where water bodies correspond to category 16, land–sea mask data were extracted from meteorological input files (met_em). The marine grid boundaries were accurately identified through geographic coordinate transformation (WRF Lambert projection to WGS84 coordinate system). A series of high-resolution SST data from the CMIP6 were spatially and temporally aligned with WRF marine domains using a spatiotemporal matching algorithm (Supplementary Fig. 6). Daily SST fields were dynamically integrated into the WRF initial conditions, replacing static SST parameters. Daily and diurnal SST in the future under SSP 245 and SSP 585 scenarios were applied to examine the effect of future ocean warming on SLB days in the same way as introduced above for historical case. With the advancement of remote sensing and multisource data assimilation techniques, ultrahigh-resolution SST products (for example, GHRSST) provide opportunities for cross-validation using several datasets. Our comparison shows that reanalysis data exhibit the highest correlation with the BCC simulations, followed by satellite observations from GHRSST42 (Supplementary Text 5). Residual biases in SLB-day magnitude may arise from uncertainties in nearshore SST fields and coastal wind representation, especially for cities with complex coastlines.Model configurationsThe WRF model is a mesoscale weather model and assimilation system. It is widely adopted by most countries around the world as a tool for both operational and research applications in mesoscale weather forecasting. The WRF model used in this study is v.4.2.2, with the specific physical process parameterization settings (Supplementary Text 6).This study used a controlled-variable experimental design to conduct sensitivity analyses focused on SST, using an enhanced high-resolution SST modelling result coupled with the WRF model. Simulations conducted under control parameterization with fixed land use, anthropogenic heat and surface roughness, took identical meteorological fields as initial and boundary conditions. Comparative analysis of SLB days changes under different SST conditions quantitatively revealed the impact of anomalous SST warming on SLB patterns.The simulation period spans from 1 January to 31 December for a total of 365 days each year. A 22-days model spin-up was implemented to optimize computational efficiency and minimize initial field interference under the guidance of several initial experiments. Two nested domains were used with spatial resolutions of 27 km and 9 km (Supplementary Text 7). SST data in the years 1970, 2010 and 2050 (SSP245 and SSP585) simulated by BCC-CSM2-MR model were used for forcing at 6-h intervals.In selecting the points of interest for this study, we considered previous research43 and factors such as city size, latitude and longitude distribution, the continent they are located on and the climate zone they represent. Our goal was to achieve comprehensive coverage to discuss the potential impacts of SST changes on SLB from several perspectives (Supplementary Text 6 and Supplementary Table 1). To select research regions, this study identified global climatic zones predominantly influenced by SLB first. The night-time light data guided the selection of megacities with the highest light intensity indices within these climate zones (Fig. 1)41,44. Final spot selection integrated several criteria, including urban size, prevailing wind patterns, coastal proximity and climatic characteristics. Urban–coastal boundaries were delineated using land-use data, with two nested domains configured to centre on urban land areas adjacent to the coastline. The inner domain was designed to maintain near-equal representation of land and ocean (~50% each), ensuring balanced atmospheric interactions. This balanced configuration minimizes disproportionate boundary condition influences from any single peripheral direction while reducing potential biases introduced by human intervention in experimental design.Data collectionHigh-resolution SST data from the CMIP6 (BCC-CSM2-MR model, 0.3°–1° spatial resolution from the equator towards the poles)45 has been used in this research. The historical SST simulation data from BCC spans from 1950 to 2014. The study also selected data for 2050 under two emission scenarios, SSP 245 and SSP 585, to analyse the changes in SLB under different future emission scenarios. Another four high-resolution SST datasets from CMIP6 were used in this study, namely ACCESS-ESM1.5 (~1.25° × 1.875°), CanESM5 (~2.8° × 2.8°), CESM2 (~1.25° × 0.9°) and EC-Earth3 (~1.0° × 1.0°), to evaluate how intermodel differences in SST affect the simulation outcomes. For meteorological data, this study used the final operational global analysis (FNL) dataset provided by the National Centers for Environmental Prediction46. The FNL dataset, generated by the Global Data Assimilation System, includes various meteorological parameters with a spatial resolution of 0.25° × 0.25° and is updated every 6 h. FNL data are widely used in meteorological research and numerical weather prediction models, making it a common data source for numerical simulations. To control for the influence of other variables on the simulation, all other parameters and input data were maintained at their default model settings. The land-use classification followed the MODIS land-use classification standards and simulations were based on the land-use parameters provided with WRF v.4.2.2.Criteria to identify SLB dayAccording to the basic definition, SLB day must meet the criteria that both sea breeze and land breeze occur in one day with obvious conversion process observed29. Moreover, only the day meeting all following criteria including the difference in temperature, specific wind direction, wind speed limitation as well as the duration intensity can be identified as an SLB day in this study. The sea breeze was defined as the case when the wind direction fits the sea breeze area and the temperature in the marine observatory was lower than that in the urban observatory. Similarly, the land breeze was defined as the case when the wind direction fit the land breeze area and the temperature in the marine observatory was higher than that in the urban observatory (Supplementary Table 1). Although the above two conditions satisfy the basic definition of SLB day, this method does not use a specific time for the determination of sea breeze or land breeze as described in the previous studies. This method excluded cases with observed wind speed greater than 10 m s−1 in the coastal observatory to prevent disturbances by large-scale weather systems. The time and intensity of SLB were considered in this method for the identification of SLB day. For a typical SLB day, both sea breeze and land breeze must occur for more than 4 h a day. In addition, the wind speed of sea breeze should be greater than 1 m s−1 and the wind speed of land breeze should be greater than 0.5 m s−1. All these criteria have a logical relevance. The temperature difference is the essential cause of the SLB, which is the fundamental criterion. Wind criterion includes direction and speed, which is specialized through the shape of coastline and the distribution of land and sea. After that, the duration intensity is considered to make sure the SLB is obvious.Unsupervised cluster analysisThis investigation used unsupervised machine learning (k-means clustering) to delineate SST variability–sea breeze day frequency relationships, identifying three distinct coastal response clusters. The clustering analysis was achieved by constructing two-dimensional feature vectors from SST anomalies and sea breeze day frequency, which geometrically characterized the data. Iterative computation of Euclidean distances between data points and cluster centroids assigned samples to clusters on the basis of a minimum distance threshold. To determine the optimal cluster number (k) without prior knowledge of class categories, the evaluating the sum-of-squared-errors method was applied. Through several iterations, k = 3 was identified as the optimal classification number, balancing computational efficiency and cluster stability. The specific mathematical models used are as follows:$$J=\mathop{\sum }\limits_{i=1}^{k}\mathop{\sum }\limits_{{x}_{j}\in {C}_{i}}{\mathrm{||}{x}_{i}-{\mu }_{i}\mathrm{||}}^{2}$$
                    (1)
                $${\mu }_{i}=\frac{1}{\left|{C}_{i}\right|}\mathop{\sum }\limits_{{x}_{j}\in {C}_{i}}{x}_{j}$$
                    (2)
                $${C}_{i}=\left\{\begin{array}{c}\left.{x}_{i}\right|{\rm{d}}\left({x}_{i},{\mu }_{{\rm{i}}}\right)\le {\rm{d}}\left({x}_{i},{\mu }_{k}\right),\forall k\ne {\rm{i}}\}\end{array}\right.$$
                    (3)
                The objective function J defined in equation (1), quantifies the total within-cluster variance as the sum of squared Euclidean distances between each data point xj and its corresponding cluster centroid μi. k is the number of clusters, Ci means the ith cluster, containing all data points assigned to it. The data point in the feature space is xj and μi centroid (mean position) of the ith cluster, calculated iteratively.In equation (2), |Ci| denotes the cardinality (number of data points) within-cluster Ci. This metric reflects the statistical weight of the cluster centroid μi, quantifying its capacity to represent the spatial distribution of elements in Ci during each iteration, equation (3) assigns every data point xj to its nearest cluster centroid Ci.We further validated the clustering outcome using a Monte Carlo resampling method. The average silhouette coefficient across all iterations was 0.82, confirming the overall validity and stability of the identified clusters (Supplementary Text 8).Spatiotemporal statistical downscaling methodThe method to generate dynamically consistent atmospheric and boundary conditions derived from CMIP6 outputs. This approach enables each future scenario to evolve under its corresponding large-scale atmospheric state, rather than assuming static IC/BC fields. Specifically, we established a variable mapping between CMIP6 datasets and WRF input files (Supplementary Text 4 and Supplementary Table 3). On the basis of the temporal stamps in the WRF files, we used a nearest-neighbour temporal matching algorithm to align CMIP6 time slices, followed by bilinear spatial interpolation to transform CMIP6 data from the native grid to the WRF domain. Special longitude adjustments were applied to ensure continuity across the dateline (0°–360° and −180°–180° systems). For vertical interpolation, we performed a log-pressure remapping of pressure-level variables, maintaining monotonicity and ensuring physical consistency across layers. Comprehensive dimensional checks, alignment verification and post-interpolation validation were also implemented to confirm physical plausibility of the downscaled fields.

Data availability
            
            The model used in this study is WRF v.4.2.2, which can be downloaded from https://github.com/wrf-model/WRF/releases/tag/v4.2.2. The FNL dataset provided by the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR), is accessible at https://rda.ucar.edu/datasets/d083002/ (ref. 46). ERA5 reanalysis is available at https://www.ecmwf.int/en/forecasts/datasets/reanalysis-datasets/era5. MODIS land-cover data are available at https://ladsweb.modaps.eosdis.nasa.gov/missions-and-measurements/products/MCD12Q1. CMIP6 model outputs are available at https://aims2.llnl.gov/search/cmip6/ (ref. 45). The GHRSST dataset is available at https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/oceans/ghrsst/L4/GLOB/REMSS/mw_ir_OI/. We have made the numerical modelling dataset, comprising simulation outputs and other resources available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15256832 (ref. 47).
          Code availability
            
            Statistical analyses are performed using NCAR Command Language (NCL) (v.6.6.2). The software can be downloaded and installed from https://www.ncl.ucar.edu/current_release.shtml. The computer code necessary to reproduce the main results are available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18932849 (ref. 48).

          Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended dataExtended Data Fig. 1 Interannual variation in the number of SLB days in major coastal cities based on ERA5 reanalysis data.The annual frequency of SLB days across 18 coastal megacities labelled from (a) Tianjin to (r) Jakarta, with blue dots showing observed values and red dashed lines indicating linear trends. Each subpanel represents a city: (a) Tianjin, (b) Shanghai, (c) Shenzhen, (d) Tokyo, (e) Melbourne, (f) Los Angeles, (g) Miami, (h) New York, (i) Buenos Aires, (j) Rio de Janeiro, (k) London, (l) Lisbon, (m) Douala, (n) Cape Town, (o) Maputo, (p) Dubai, (q) Mumbai, and (r) Jakarta. From 2001 to 2020, SLB days decrease in 16 cities, except for (n) Cape Town and (p) Dubai. Across declining coastal megacities, the mean rate of change is −1.36 days per year. Pronounced decreases with relatively strong linear relationships (R2 > 0.4) are evident in (a) Tianjin, (b) Shanghai, (c) Shenzhen, (i) Buenos Aires, and (l) Lisbon. These results provide important observational constraints for subsequent numerical simulations.Extended Data Fig. 2 Comparison of SLB days and attribution under different emission scenarios.(a) Number of SLB days in high impact regions under present day (2010; blue), SSP245 (2050; red), and SSP585 (2050; magenta) conditions. Sub figures b and c relative contributions of thermal contrast (SLTD), circulation intensity (SLCI), and wind-related factors (SLWF) to SLB weakening under SSP245 (b) and SSP585 (c) scenarios. Simulations under realistic scenarios show that the amplifying effect of high emission forcing on the weakening of SLB remains clearly detectable. Compared with 2010, SLB days decrease by 6.06% under SSP245 and by 23.85% under SSP585, temperature differences remain the dominant factor driving SLB decline in both scenarios.Supplementary informationSupplementary Information (download PDF )Supplementary Figs. 1–16, Tables 1–5, Texts 1–8 and references.Rights and permissions

items.title: A Torino la Giornata della Terra 2026, tra talk, workshop e tante attività
items.description: A pochi giorni dall'Earth day, torna la Giornata della Terra di Torino, appuntamento ormai fisso per parlare di buone pratiche sostenibili.
items.body:
aprile
		
			A Torino la Giornata della Terra 2026, tra talk, workshop e tante attività
		
		
			A pochi giorni dall’Earth day, torna la Giornata della Terra di Torino, appuntamento ormai fisso per parlare di buone pratiche sostenibili.
		
					
									Torino
											 Evento

Earth day, cos'è la Giornata della Terra e perché si celebra

Earth day 2021, gli impegni presi al summit organizzato dagli Stati Uniti
			
			
				Al summit americano per l’Earth day hanno partecipato più di 40 paesi tra cui Cina, Russia, India e Brasile. Alcune dichiarazioni hanno fatto scalpore.

Clima

22 aprile 2021

Tempesta Vaia. La voce di chi sta lavorando per far rinascere i boschi del Trentino
			
			
				Per l’Earth Day 2021 abbiamo intervistato Paolo Kovatsch, responsabile del recupero delle aree della provincia di Trento distrutte dalla tempesta Vaia.

Ambiente

22 aprile 2021

La barriera corallina è un’opera d’arte da restaurare
			
			
				Al Marhe center dell’università Bicocca, alle Maldive, si studiano le tecniche di restauro della scogliera corallina. L’intervista al direttore Paolo Galli.

Biodiversità

22 aprile 2021

Giornata della Terra 2021, dieci esempi di ripristino degli ecosistemi
			
			
				Il tema della Giornata della Terra 2021 è il ripristino degli ecosistemi. Dal rewilding alla riforestazione, vediamo dieci esempi.

Biodiversità

21 aprile 2021

Earth day, Pollica e la dieta mediterranea protagoniste della maratona digitale Food for Earth
			
			
				La città di Pollica, capitale della dieta mediterranea, si prepara ad accogliere il 22 aprile la maratona digitale Food for Earth. L’editoriale del sindaco.

Cibo e terra

20 aprile 2021

Cos’è il ripristino degli ecosistemi, la sfida a cui l’Onu ha dedicato questo decennio
			
			
				La Giornata dell’ambiente 2021 ci esorta ad agire per il ripristino degli ecosistemi danneggiati dall’uomo. Un tema a cui l’Onu ha dedicato il decennio.

Glossario del clima

20 aprile 2021

items.title: 10 lupi uccisi nel Parco Nazionale d’Abruzzo, Lazio e Molise
items.description: Uno dei più gravi crimini di natura degli ultimi 10 anni e una deriva criminale inaccettabile in un paese civile. Il declassamento dello stato del lup
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie Uno dei più gravi crimini di natura degli ultimi 10 anni Profonda e crescente preoccupazione per la notizia del rinvenimento di cinque lupi morti ad Alfedena, nell’area contigua del Parco Nazionale d’Abruzzo, Lazio e Molise, che si aggiungono ad altri cinque esemplari trovati senza vita nei giorni scorsi a Pescasseroli, nel cuore dell’area protetta. Siamo davanti a uno dei più gravi crimini di natura degli ultimi 10 anni. L’ipotesi dell’avvelenamento Secondo i primi accertamenti, l’ipotesi più accreditata è quella dell’avvelenamento, un crimine che non risparmia nessuna specie. Il veleno resta uno degli atti criminali più vigliacchi, in quanto colpisce indiscriminatamente specie selvatiche, animali domestici e potenzialmente anche gli esseri umani. Per il WWF nessun conflitto con le attività umane e nessun danno economico possono giustificare simili atti criminali. Il lupo rappresenta un patrimonio indiscusso della biodiversità italiana e svolge un ruolo fondamentale nel mantenimento degli equilibri naturali. Colpirlo in una delle aree simbolo della sua conservazione significa non solo uccidere animali protetti, ma persino compromettere la salute degli ecosistemi e cancellare decenni di impegno nella tutela della natura. Il declassamento dello stato del lupo incentiva il bracconaggio Questo episodio si inserisce in un quadro sempre più preoccupante. L’uccisione di questi dieci lupi segue di pochi giorni altri gravissimi episodi avvenuti in Toscana, dove due esemplari sono stati uccisi e mutilati. Una sequenza che evidenzia una deriva criminale sempre più dilagante sul territorio nazionale. Questo clima di odio nei confronti del lupo è alimentato anche da decisioni politiche che ne indeboliscono la tutela, a partire dal recente declassamento del suo stato di protezione. Una scelta che, come più volte evidenziato, non contribuirà a ridurre la mortalità illegale, ma rischia al contrario di legittimare comportamenti illegali e incentivare il bracconaggio, come drammaticamente confermato dagli eventi di queste settimane. In caso di processo il WWF si costituirà parte civile In attesa dei risultati definitivi delle necroscopie affidate all’IZS di Abruzzo e Molise, il WWF chiede il massimo impegno per l’individuazione dei responsabili. Le indagini sono coordinate dalla Procura della Repubblica di Sulmona. In caso di auspicato rinvio a giudizio, il WWF si riserva di costituirsi parte civile nel processo per questo gravissimo crimine di natura. È oggi necessario un intervento immediato e deciso, che miri a rafforzare i controlli sul territorio, intensificare le attività investigative e garantire che i responsabili vengano individuati e perseguiti. Allo stesso tempo, è fondamentale investire nella prevenzione dei conflitti e nel sostegno alle comunità locali, promuovendo una convivenza possibile e sostenibile tra uomo e grandi carnivori. In un Paese civile non è accettabile assistere a episodi simili. Difendere il lupo oggi non significa solo proteggere una specie, ma equivale a difendere la legalità, la sicurezza dei territori e il futuro della biodiversità nel nostro Paese. Adotta un lupo Aiutaci a proteggere questa specie iconica Adotta Lupo Scopri di più Stop crimini di natura Per fermare davvero i crimini di natura servono strumenti adeguati ma da troppo tempo lo Stato non investe in questo settore Scopri di più

The catalogue contains a comprehensive list of information products relevant to the various Copernicus services.

Learn more

On January 27, 2025, clumps of solidified vegetable oil were found along a 25 km stretch of coastline on Finland’s Hanko Peninsula. While not toxic, the substance posed risks to wildlife. Suspecting it came from a passing vessel’s cargo tank wash, Finnish authorities launched an investigation,…

The COSNORTH-SEMAR DSS is a decision-support system developed to enhance maritime surveillance and law enforcement along Spain’s northern coast. Created in collaboration with the Guardia Civil, it combines environmental data, vessel tracking, and predictive modeling in a user-friendly platform…

Planning a sailing route based on weather used to be hard and needed expert skills. Many sailors didn’t use it because it was too complicated. SailRouting changes that by offering an easy online tool that shows weather-based routes in just a few clicks — no setup needed.

With SailRouting…

This study examines the environmental conditions near the Motagua River mouth in the Caribbean Sea, focusing on mangrove resilience, plastic pollution, and water turbidity. Photosynthesis data reveals magrove strong resistance with high absorption values in northern and southern regions, though…

The Scandinavian Peninsula spans 🇳🇴 🇸🇪 🇫🇮, where early spring brings melting in the south and persistent snow in the north.🛰️ This #CopernicusEU Sentinel-3 image (11 Apr) shows snow-covered mountains, fjords and thawing landscapes.🔗 https://t.co/d0fuE9Z6lz#ImageOfTheDay pic.twitter.com/vWKTXf2SmJ

— Copernicus EU (@CopernicusEU) April 14, 2026

🏙️ Cabo San Lucas 🇲🇽 is rapidly expanding.🛰️ #CopernicusEU Sentinel-2 images (2017 vs 2026) show clear urban growth into surrounding desert areas, especially along road networks.🔗 https://t.co/pKQrDOLOtN#ImageOfTheDay pic.twitter.com/gVbHpFNBW6

— Copernicus EU (@CopernicusEU) April 13, 2026

items.title: Humanitarian blind spots in Western climate change policy and discourse
Clima, appello ai parlamentari italiani verso la conferenza di Santa Marta
items.description: Ong ecologiste, movimenti e sindacati hanno inviato un appello ai rappresentanti italiani chiedendo un impegno chiaro sull’uscita dai combustibili fos
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie Non solo un appello alle istituzioni, ma l’avvio di una mobilitazione ampia e diffusa. In vista della Conferenza internazionale sulla transizione oltre i combustibili fossili, che si terrà a Santa Marta, in Colombia, dal 24 al 29 aprile 2026, una vasta rete di organizzazioni della società civile italiana ha deciso di attivarsi per sostenere questo appuntamento cruciale per il futuro climatico globale. In questo quadro, ong ecologiste, movimenti e sindacati hanno inviato un appello a deputati, senatori ed europarlamentari italiani chiedendo un impegno chiaro e coerente sull’uscita dai combustibili fossili e un ruolo attivo dell’Italia e dell’Unione europea nel rafforzare l’ambizione climatica internazionale. L’appello è promosso da una rete ampia e trasversale di realtà impegnate nella difesa dell’ambiente, del clima e dei diritti: A Sud, CGIL, Ecora, Extinction Rebellion Italia, Fridays for Future Italia, Greenpeace Italia, Legambiente, Osservatorio Parigi, Per il clima fuori dal fossile, VAS – Verdi Ambiente e Società, WWF Italia. Un appuntamento storico a livello globale Promossa dai governi di Colombia e Paesi Bassi, la Conferenza rappresenta un appuntamento storico a livello globale: il primo dedicato alla costruzione di una coalizione internazionale per accelerare una transizione energetica giusta, equa e in linea con gli obiettivi dell’Accordo di Parigi, che non può che partire dalla definizione di tempistiche certe e obiettivi misurabili per l’abbandono delle fonti fossili. L’iniziativa è collegata alla Fossil Fuel Treaty Initiative, un’alleanza tra Stati e società civile che promuove l’adozione di un trattato internazionale giuridicamente vincolante per la progressiva eliminazione di carbone, petrolio e gas. L’obiettivo è dotare la comunità internazionale di uno strumento capace di governare il phase-out dei combustibili fossili e costruire un futuro sicuro e sostenibile. Un passaggio cruciale, mentre le emissioni globali continuano a crescere e la possibilità di contenere il riscaldamento entro 1,5°C si riduce rapidamente. Nell’Appello, le organizzazioni denunciano l’insufficienza delle politiche climatiche attuali, sia a livello globale che europeo e nazionale. In Italia, sottolineano, continua a emergere una distanza sempre più evidente tra gli impegni internazionali e le scelte politiche concrete. Il ruolo dell’Italia A rendere questa contraddizione ancora più evidente è un fatto politico rilevante: secondo comunicazioni ufficiali della presidenza colombiana, il Governo Italiano avrebbe confermato la partecipazione alla Conferenza di Santa Marta. Una notizia che, tuttavia, non trova riscontro né sui canali ufficiali dei ministeri competenti né nel dibattito pubblico nazionale, alimentando interrogativi sulla trasparenza del Governo e sulla reale posizione che l’Italia intende assumere a livello internazionale. Le politiche energetiche del governo, poi, appaiono contraddire gli impegni assunti in sede internazionale, a partire dalla “transizione fuori dai combustibili fossili” decisa a Dubai nel 2023. L’Italia ha infatti recentemente rinviato il phase-out dal carbone dal 2025 al 2038, posticipandolo di tredici anni rispetto agli obiettivi precedentemente fissati, riaprendo la possibilità di utilizzo del combustibile più inquinante e motivando la decisione con esigenze di sicurezza energetica. Allo stesso tempo, il governo italiano ha sostenuto a livello europeo la necessità di allentare o sospendere il sistema ETS (Emission Trading System), uno degli strumenti chiave della politica climatica europea, indicando chiaramente una direzione orientata alla riduzione dei vincoli ambientali piuttosto che al loro rafforzamento. Serve una giusta transizione In conclusione , le politiche energetiche nazionali continuano a essere fortemente orientate al mantenimento e al prolungamento della dipendenza dalle fonti fossili, con nuovi investimenti nel gas e rallentamenti nello sviluppo delle energie rinnovabili e dell’efficienza energetica. A questo si aggiunge un contesto geopolitico segnato da conflitti sempre più legati al controllo delle risorse fossili e da una crescente riallocazione delle risorse pubbliche verso il riarmo, a scapito degli investimenti nella transizione ecologica e nella protezione delle comunità più vulnerabili. E’ proprio di questi giorni l’uscita dell’AD di ENI Descalzi che, di fronte alla crisi energetica scaturita dall’attacco all’Iran da parte di Stati Uniti e Israele, suggerisce di riaprire i rifornimenti di gas e petrolio russo, una proposta irricevibile, tesa a costringere a rimanere incatenato ai combustibili fossili e che conferma ancora una volta il legame tra conflitti, dittature e fossili. L’uscita dalle fossili passa invece per una giusta transizione e per l’investimento strutturale verso un sistema basato sul risparmio energetico e sulle fonti rinnovabili, sistema su cui investono alcuni paesi come la Spagna, traendone grandi benefici anche in termini di vantaggi concreti per le bollette di famiglie e imprese. Gli appuntamenti della mobilitazione Per questo,  le organizzazioni firmatarie dell’appello chiedono alle rappresentanze parlamentari italiane ed europee di attivarsi concretamente, utilizzando tutti gli strumenti istituzionali a disposizione. In particolare, l’appello invita a: interrogare il governo sul mandato con cui parteciperà alla Conferenza; a sostenere il percorso politico della Conferenza e delle proposte che ne emergeranno nelle sedi nazionali ed europee; promuovere nel dibattito pubblico l’urgenza di una rapida uscita dai combustibili fossili; rafforzare politiche coerenti con una transizione energetica giusta; costruire una coalizione europea ad alta ambizione climatica. Le organizzazioni firmatarie chiedono inoltre l’apertura di un confronto diretto con parlamentari italiani ed europei, prima e dopo la Conferenza, per contribuire alla definizione di iniziative politiche capaci di rafforzare l’azione climatica e la cooperazione internazionale. “In un momento storico segnato da crisi climatiche, guerre e disuguaglianze crescenti – si legge nell’appello – l’uscita dai combustibili fossili non è solo una necessità ambientale, ma una condizione per la giustizia globale e la costruzione di percorsi di pace”. La mobilitazione della società civile proseguirà nelle prossime settimane con un’iniziativa pubblica il 21 aprile all’Università La Sapienza di Roma (ore 17.00 presso l’Aula C della Facolà di Chimica) e con un presidio di piazza che si terrà a Piazza Capranica alle 11.00 27 aprile, alla vigilia della sessione di negoziazioni governative che concluderà la Conferenza di Santa Marta, per rilanciare la pressione politica e sociale verso una rapida uscita dai combustibili fossili. 4 Aprile 2024 Accelerare la transizione energetica dell'acciaio L’acciaio a zero emissioni? Si può fare ed ed è urgente accelerarne la transizione per rispettare gli impegni di neutralità climatica In primo piano 23 Dicembre 2025 Il bilancio del 2025 in Europa, da Green a Black in 8 mosse Dalla deforestazione al declassamento del lupo, il bilancio del WWF sui passi indietro delle istituzioni europee nella tutela ambientale In primo piano 12 Giugno 2023 100% rinnovabili al 2035, si può Presentato oggi a Roma uno studio commissionato da Greenpeace Italia, Legambiente e WWF Italia 11 Novembre 2025 Giustizia climatica, il 15 novembre torna a Roma il Climate Pride Oltre settanta associazioni, movimenti e collettivi parteciperanno alla street parade colorata che attraverserà la Capitale

items.title: ARPAT: emergenze ambientali nel 2025
items.description: Dal 2014 la Sala Operativa della Protezione Civile (SOP) della Città Metropolitana di Firenze costituisce la sala operativa ARPAT a livello regionale
items.body:
Home
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News ed Eventi
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News
                    
                            
                            
                                                ARPAT: emergenze ambientali nel 2025

ARPAT: emergenze ambientali nel 2025

Temi ambientali:
                                                                                                            Emergenze ambientali
                                                                                                
                        
                                                                                                                
                                    Tag:
                                                                            Acque superficiali,                                                                            Incendio,                                                                            Sversamento                                                                    
                                                    
                                    
            
            
            
                
                                            Pubblicato il:
                        
                            15 Aprile 2026

Condividi

Condividi

                    Condividi su Whatsapp

Gli interventi in emergenza effettuati, nel 2025, da ARPAT a seguito di attivazione da parte della Sala operativa della Protezione civile della Città Metropolitana di Firenze

Dal 2014 la Sala Operativa della Protezione Civile (SOP) della Città Metropolitana di Firenze costituisce la sala operativa ARPAT a livello regionale per le emergenze ambientali; ad essa, quindi, si rivolgono tutti gli Enti competenti e le Polizie del territorio della Regione Toscana per chiedere il supporto dell’Agenzia in caso di eventi che impattano o possono impattare sull’ambiente.
La collaborazione con la SOP della Città Metropolitana di Firenze è tra le più sviluppate, integrate e proficue del territorio italiano, tanto che ARPAT, insieme alle agenzie ambientali della Lombardia e del Veneto, fa parte del Gruppo Esperto per la realizzazione di un sistema informativo integrato WebGis di supporto alle attività in emergenza delle componenti del Sistema Nazionale per la Protezione dell’Ambiente (SNPA).
Proprio il SNPA ha da poco prodotto il primo rapporto di Sistema dedicato alle attivazioni in contesti emergenziali, elaborato sulla base dei dati 2024, che costituire la base per l’implementazione di un sistema di osservazione continuo, finalizzato al miglioramento progressivo delle azioni del Sistema in ambito emergenziale.
Emergenze in Toscana nel 2025: le richieste di intervento
Le richieste di intervento ricevute dalla SOP nel corso del 2025 sono state 177, in linea con i dati degli ultimi anni (192 nel 2022, 174 nel 2023, 181 nel 2024), e provengono per lo più dai Vigili del Fuoco (33,9%,), in aumento rispetto agli anni precedenti, seguiti dalla Polizia municipale con il 32,8%, valore che, insieme all’attivazione da parte di uffici comunali diversi, sale al 40,7%. Seguono i Carabinieri Forestali con il 13%; le altre forze di Polizia, quali Carabinieri, Polizia di Stato, Polizia Provinciale e Capitaneria di Porto, assommano a circa il 7,9% delle chiamate totali, in diminuzione rispetto agli anni precedenti (12,7% nel 2024).
ARPAT, anche quando avvisata da altri enti, di norma chiede l’attivazione e la collaborazione in particolare della Polizia municipale che può raggiungere i luoghi di evento in tempi brevi, costituendo quindi un valido interlocutore sia nelle prime fasi, in assenza di personale dell’Agenzia in campo, ma anche successivamente durante la gestione dell’evento, essendo fra l’altro personale a diretto contatto con la locale autorità competente.
La percentuale di chiamate nella fascia oraria di pronta disponibilità, ovvero in orario notturno (dopo le 19,30 e fino alle 7,30) o festivo, mostra un leggero decremento rispetto al 2024, anche se i valori dal 2018 sono in linea.
Per quanto riguarda i territori provinciali più coinvolti, Firenze risulta essere quello dove si è registrato il numero maggiore di chiamate (39), seguita da Pistoia (21), Livorno (20), Arezzo e Lucca (19), Pisa e Siena (17).
ARPAT ha fornito supporto in più dell’80% dei casi, in netto aumento rispetto agli anni precedenti
Nel 2025 sono stati effettuati dall’Agenzia 144 interventi con sopralluogo o con supporto da remoto, pari al 81,4% delle attivazioni ricevute, il dato più alto dal 2017, a testimonianza dell’appropriatezza delle chiamate inoltrate ad ARPAT tramite il sistema di emergenza.
Solo il 3,4% delle telefonate arrivate alla SOP non è risultato di competenza dell’Agenzia, confermando il calo già rilevato negli anni precedenti; anche da questo dato è possibile confermare l’appropriatezza delle chiamate in entrata al servizio emergenze dell’Agenzia.
Per quanto riguarda i 144 interventi

nel 76% dei casi ARPAT è intervenuta sul campo
nel 16% dei casi è intervenuta con supporto da remoto
nel 8% dei casi l’intervento dell’Agenzia è stato procrastinato

Durante il 35% degli interventi sono stati effettuati campionamenti e nel 28% dei casi è stato necessario effettuare attività di post emergenza.
Tipologie di emergenze: oltre il 52% degli interventi riguarda sversamenti
Oltre il 52% degli interventi di ARPAT hanno riguardato sversamenti in matrici ambientali varie; nel corso dell’anno abbiamo raccontato alcuni degli interventi di ARPAT nei casi di sversamento, attraverso specifiche notizie pubblicate sul sito Web:

20 giugno Intervento di ARPAT per sversamento di acido cloridrico da una cisterna a Rosignano Solvay (LI)
16 luglio Presenza di schiuma nel torrente Pescia (PT)
13 agosto Intervento ARPAT per sversamento idrocarburi a Marina di Pietrasanta (LU)
15 settembre Presenza di gasolio nel rio Santovecchio a Pescia (PT)
23 ottobre Sversamento di oli in acque superficiali a Capannori (LU)
29 ottobre Sversamento di liquido lattiginoso nel fosso Ponticello a Caldine, Fiesole (FI)
6 novembre Presenza di idrocarburi presso un cantiere ad Arezzo
16 novembre Sversamento idrocarburi a Montecarlo (LU)

Gli incendi hanno interessato oltre il 37% degli interventi e i cattivi odori oltre il 15%.
Alcune notizie pubblicate sul sito Web hanno raccontato questi eventi:

28 giugno Rosignano (LI): prime valutazioni di ARPAT a seguito dell’incendio alla discarica di Scapigliato
18 luglio Stagno (LI), prende fuoco un rimorchio contenente rifiuti sulla FI-PI-LI
18 agosto Incendio presso Relife Recycling Srl di Capannori: primi sopralluoghi di ARPAT
23 agosto Incendio presso il Viper Theatre a Firenze: sopralluoghi di ARPAT
13 ottobre Incendio presso ditta Frosini a Signa (FI): l’intervento di ARPAT
17 ottobre Cortona (AR), incendio alla MB Elettronica

Le principali matrici coinvolte dalle emergenze: i corpi idrici i più impattati
Per ciascun tipo di emergenza, sono individuate le matrici ambientali impattate, in alcuni casi l’impatto si ha su un’unica matrice in altri su più. Ancora una volta la matrice maggiormente impattata è quella dei corpi idrici superficiali, seguita da aria e suolo.
Concludiamo, ricordando che il numero con cui viene attivata ARPAT tramite la SOP non è disponibile per i cittadini che, se vogliono segnalare un’emergenza ambientale, possono chiamare la Polizia municipale del Comune dove la problematica è in corso o altre forze di polizia o quando necessario i Vigili del Fuoco.

Potrebbero interessarti anche

items.title: Accesso agli atti e alle informazioni ambientali: in costante crescita le richieste ad ARPAT
items.description: Le richieste di accesso agli atti e alle informazioni ambientali rappresentano uno strumento fondamentale di partecipazione e trasparenza nel rapporto
items.body:
Home
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News ed Eventi
                    
                            
                            
                                                                    
                        News
                    
                            
                            
                                                Accesso agli atti e alle informazioni ambientali: in costante crescita le richieste ad ARPAT

Accesso agli atti e alle informazioni ambientali: in costante crescita le richieste ad ARPAT

Tag:
                                                                            Comunicazione e informazione                                                                    
                                                    
                                    
            
            
            
                
                                            Pubblicato il:
                        
                            10 Aprile 2026

Condividi
